Facts.text: Martyrdom of Saint Adrian of Nicomedia, a Roman guard, who is beheaded for converting to Christianity under Roman Emperor Galerius
Croatian Duke Trpimir I issued a statute, a document with the first known written mention of the Croats name in Croatian sources
Translation of the relics of martyr Wenceslaus I, Duke of Bohemia, the patron saint of the Czech state
Battle of the Sit River: Mongol forces of Batu Khan overcome Russians under Yuri II of Vladimir-Suzdal near Yaroslavl in Russia, ending Russian resistance
Ramathibodi becomes King of Siam
Władysław II Jagiełło (Jogaila) crowned King of Poland
KingPhilip IIbans foreign Dutch students as part of his attempted suppression of Protestantism
Maurice of Nassau's ship reaches Breda
George Abbot appointed Archbishop of Canterbury
Jacarta, Java, is renamed Batavia
Dutch commodore Johan van Galen leads a Dutch fleet to victory against an English naval force at the Battle of Leghorn (Livorno) during the Anglo Dutch wars, later dies from his wounds on March 23
Jews are expelled from residing in Lübeck, Germany
British fleet under Rear Admiral of the Blue Sir Chaloner Ogle reaches Cartagena de Indias (Colombia)
American War of Independence: The Americans capture Dorchester Heights dominating the port of Boston, Massachusetts
US House of Representatives holds its first full meeting at Federal Hall in NYC. Frederick Muhlenberg is elected as first speaker.
France is divided into 83 départements, which cut across the former provinces in an attempt to dislodge regional loyalties based on noble ownership of land
First Jewish member of US Congress, Israel Jacobs (PA), takes office
President Washington calls the US Senate into its first special session
Vermont admitted as 14th state (1st addition to the 13 colonies)
Oranges introduced to Hawaii
French troops conquer Geertruidenberg, Netherlands

The Battle of Vinegar Hill, colony of New South Wales (Australia), when Irish convicts (some of whom had been involved in Ireland's Battle of Vinegar Hill in 1798) led the colony's only significant convict uprising.
Boston residents eligible to vote ratify the incorporation of the city and accept the charter to convert their town into a city
The "National Institution for the Preservation of Life from Shipwreck" founded in the United Kingdom, later to be renamed The Royal National Lifeboat Institution in 1858.
1st US railroad chartered, Granite Railway in Quincy, Massachusetts
Unruly crowd mobs the White House during PresidentAndrew Jackson's inaugural ball
Vincenzo Bellini's opera "I Capuleti e i Montecchi" premieres at the Teatro La Fenice in Venice, Italy
HMS Beagle moves into Bay of Concepcion (Chile)
Village of Chicago becomes incorporated as a city
Weekly Advocate changes its name to the Colored American
Carlo Alberto di Savoia signs the Statuto Albertino that will later represent the first constitution of the Regno d'Italia
Sardinia-Piemonte gets new Constitution
Pope Pius IX recovers Catholic hierarchy in Netherlands
William Rufus de Vane King (D) sworn in as 13th US vice-president
Confederate States adopt "Stars & Bars" flag (US Civil War)
US PresidentAbraham Lincolnopens Government Printing Office
Battle of Thompson's Station, Tennessee
Territory of Idaho established
US Confederate congress approves final design of "official flag"
44th US Congress (1875-77) convenes with a Democratic majority
US Congress decides to impeach Minister of War Belknap
Tchaikovski's incomplete ballet "Zwanenmeer" premieres in Moscow
NY Daily Graphic publishes 1st half-tone engraving, by S. H. Horgan
47th US Congress (1881-83) convenes
California becomes 1st state to pass plant quarantine legislation
Sherlock Holmes & Dr. Watson begin their 1st case together in "A Study in Scarlet"
Britain's first electric trams run in East London
Francis Dhanis' Belgian Congo Free State army attacks the Lualaba, occupies Nyangwe
Grover Cleveland(D) inaugrated as 24th US President (2nd term)
Great fire in Shanghai; over 1,000 buildings destroyed
Cyclone Mahina sweeps in north of Cooktown, Queensland, with a 12 m wave that reaches up to 5 km inland - over 300 dead.
Term of George H. White, last of post-Reconstruction US congressmen, ends
William McKinleyinaugurated for his 2nd term as US president; Theodore Roosevelt serves as Vice President; his inaugural address is the first to be published in advance
American Automobile Association (AAA) is founded in Chicago
Stanley Cup, Montreal Arena, Westmount, Quebec: Montreal HC beats Winnipeg Victorias, 4-1 for a 2-1 challenge series victory

Fire at Lakeview Elementary School in Collinwood, Ohio kills 172 students and 2 teachers: boiler room blaze trapped many victims in the building, prompting changes in school design and procedures nationwide
US prohibits interstate transportation of game birds
Avalanche at Bear Creek in Rogers Pass, British Columbia, kills 58 railway line workers - Canada's worst avalanche disaster
Victor Berger (Wisc) becomes 1st socialist congressman in US
1st US law regulating the shooting of migratory birds passed
Gabriel Faure's opera "Pénélope", based on Homer's "The Odyssey", premieres at the Salle Garnie, in Monte Carlo, Monaco
NY Yankees are 1st to train outside US (Bermuda)
US Department of Commerce & Labor split into separate departments
Terek Autonomous Republic established in RSFSR (until 1921)
Last day of Julian civil calendar in Greece
Hot Springs National Park created in Arkansas
The song "Happy Birthday to You" is published by Clayton F. Summy Co
Swain's Island, a remote coral atoll, is annexed by the United States and incorporated into American Samoa
US President Coolidge's inauguration broadcast live on 21 radio stations
De Geer government in Netherlands takes office
"Bunion Run" race from LA to NYC begins; It is won by Andy Payne
National Revolutionary Party founded in Mexico by Plutarco Elías Calles (will go on to hold power until 2000)
Coolidge Dam in Arizona dedicated
Emma Fahning bowls 1st sanctioned 300 game by a woman
Terrible floods ransack Languedoc and the surrounds in south-west France, resulting in twelve departments being submerged by water and causing the death of over 700 people.
West Indies beat Australia for the 1st time, by 30 runs at SCG
Chancellor Engelbert Dollfuss dissolves Austrian parliament
Frances Perkins becomes Secretary of Labor, 1st female member of the US Cabinet
Henderson, DeSylva & Brown's "Strike Me Pink" premieres in NYC
Noordwijk soccer team forms
Easter Cross on Mt. Davidson (San Francisco) dedicated
18 Geuzen resistance fighters sentenced to death in The Hague
Chicago Black Hawks goaltender Sam LoPresti faces NHL record 83 shots in a 3-2 loss to the Bruins in Boston
The United Kingdom launches Operation Claymore on the Lofoten Islands, during World War II.
Transport #50 departs with French Jews to Maidanek/Sobibor
1st US bombing of Berlin
Anti-Germany strikes in North Italy
Finland declares war on Nazi Germany
WWJ (now WDIV) TV channel 4 in Detroit, MI (NBC) begins broadcasting
Piet Van de Pol of the Netherlands crowned world champion in billiards
Security Council of UN recommends membership for Israel
James E. Wilkins appointed 1st black US sub-cabinet member
Peter Bent Brigham Hospital in Boston, Massachusetts, announces the first successful kidney transplant
1st radio facsimile transmission sent across the continent
Gold Coast officially changes its name to Ghana ahead of its independence
The S&P 500 stock market index is introduced, replacing the S&P 90.
NASA's scientific spacecraft Pioneer 4 misses moon, enters heliocentric orbit, and becomes 2nd (US 1st) earth-launched 'artificial planet'
French freighter "La Coubre" explodes in Havana Cuba, killing 100
Paul-Henri Spaak resigns as Secretary-General of NATO
AEC announces 1st atomic power plant in Antarctica in operation
Teamsters union leader Jimmy Hoffa convicted of jury tampering
Canadian Pacific airliner explodes on landing at Tokyo, 64 die
North Sea Gas 1st pumped ashore by BP
Ice Dance Championship at Vienna won by Towler & Ford (GRB)
Ice Pairs Championship at Vienna won by Belousova & Protopopov (USSR)
Orbiting Geophysical Observatory 5 launched
London East End gang bosses twins Ronnie Kray and Reggie Kray are found guilty of murder; both will die in custody
French submarine "Eurydice" explodes off Cape Camarat in the Mediterranean, all 57 crew lost
Jacksonville is 1st college basketball team to avg 100+ pts per game
NY Rangers set then NHL record of 126 games without being shut-out
"City Command" kidnaps 4 US military men at Ankara, Turkey
Abercorn Restaurant bombing: a bomb explodes in a crowded restaurant in Belfast, killing two civilians and wounding 130
Erhard Keller (Germany) skates world record 1000m (1:18.5)
Last train run between Penrith to Keswick, UK
Libya & USSR signs cooperation treaty
15th Grammy Awards: 1st Time Ever I Saw Your Face, America
David Hares' "Knuckle" premieres in London
Educational series "The Letter People" debuts on KETC-TV (PBS) in St. Louis, Missouri
John Pezzin bowls 33 consecutive strikes at Toledo, Ohio
MLB's San Francisco Giants are bought for $8 million by Bob Lurie and Bud Herseth
1st CRAY 1 supercomputer shipped, to Los Alamos Laboratories, New Mexico
Earthquake in Romania kills 1,541
West Indian cricket fast bowler Colin Croft takes 8-29 against Pakistan in 2nd Test win at Port-of-Spain; best Test figures by a pace bowler from the West Indies
Chicago Daily News, founded in 1875, publishes last issue
200th episode of "All in the Family"
US Voyager I photo reveals Jupiter's rings
Bertha Wilson is appointed as first woman to sit on the Supreme Court of Canada
NASA launches Intelsat V satellite, no. 504
Second double hat trick in Islanders history: Bossy and D. Potvin
U.S. Public Health Service's publishes its guidelines for blood donors and AIDS
STS 51-E vehicle rolls back to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida); mission cancelled
Nero, later to become Roman Emperor, is given the title princeps iuventutis (head of the youth)
Frederick I[Barbarossa] is elected Holy Roman Emperor
King Johnof England makes an oath to the Pope as a crusader to gain the support of Innocent III
Edward Plantagenet lays claim to the throne of England asEdward IVin London
KingJames IVof Scotland concludes an alliance with France against England
Anne Boleynmakes her debut at the English court at the Green Castle pageant
Three Jesuit missionaries arrive at Mughal royal court of Fatehpur Sikri, at request of EmperorAkbarto take part in theologian discussions at the Ibadat Khana (House of Worship)
England's KingCharles Igrants a royal charter to Massachusetts Bay Colony
English KingCharles IIdeclares war on Netherlands
John Flamsteedis appointed the first Astronomer Royal of England
English QuakerWilliam Pennreceives charter fromCharles II, making him sole proprietor of colonial American territory Pennsylvania
First sighting of Orion nebula byWilliam Herschel
George Washington's second inauguration as US President at the Senate Chamber of Congress Hall, Philadelphia. The shortest inaugural speech of any president with only 133 words.
John Adamsinaugurated as 2nd President of The United States,Thomas Jefferson2nd Vice President
James Madisonbecomes 1st US President inaugurated in American-made clothes
James Monroeis inaugurated as the 5th President of the United States
John Quincy Adamsinaugurated as the 6th US President
Andrew Jacksoninaugurated as 7th US President
Martin Van Bureninaugurated as the 8th President of the United States
Dion Boucicault's stage comedy "London Assurance" opens at Theatre Royal, Covent Garden, London
Longest US presidential inauguration speech (8,443 words) byWilliam Henry Harrison
James K. Polkinaugurated as 11th US President

US PresidentZachary Taylordelays his swearing-in and inauguration ceremony for one day due to religious reasons, leading to the false belief by some that Senator David Atchison (President pro tempore) technically assumed the office of President for one day
Abraham Lincolnis inaugurated as the 16th US President
Ulysses S. Grantinaugurated as 18th US President

On command ofLouis Riel, Thomas Scott is executed by a firing squad. Riel rejects all appeals and requests to intervene in an attempt to demonstrate to the Canadian government that the Métis must be taken seriously
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's ballet "Swan Lake" has its world premiere, performed by the Bolshoi Ballet in Moscow
James A. Garfieldinaugurated as the 20th President of the United States of America
South African politicianPaul Krugeraccepts ceasefire during First Boer War
Grover Clevelandinaugrated as 1st US Democratic President since Civil War
Benjamin Harrisoninaugurated as 23rd US President
Longest bridge in Great Britain, theForth Bridge(railway) at 1,710 ft in length is opened in Scotland by the Prince of Wales, later KingEdward VII
Gustav Mahlerconducts the premiere of his incomplete 2nd Symphony ("Resurrection") in Berlin, Germany, with the Berlin Philharmonic; the complete version debuts in December
William McKinleyinaugurated as 25th US President
Gerhart Hauptmann's play "Elga" premieres in Berlin
Elihu Rootbegins his tenure after being elected United States Senator for New York
William Howard Taftinaugrated as 27th US President during 10" snowstorm
Woodrow Wilsoninaugurated as 28th US President
Warren G. Hardingis inaugurated as the 29th President of the United States
First vampire film "Nosferatu," an unauthorized adaptation ofBram Stoker's Dracula, premieres at the Berlin Zoological Garden in Germany
Vladimir Lenin's last article in Pravda (about Soviet bureaucracy)
Babe Ruthbecomes the highest-paid player in MLB history when he signs 3-year, $70,000 per season contract with the New York Yankees
Herbert Hooverinaugurated as the 31st US President
RepublicanCharles Curtisbecomes the 1st Native American to be Vice President of the United States
Franklin D. Rooseveltis inaugurated as the 32nd US President, pledges to pull the US out of the Depression, stating in his inaugural address "that the only thing we have to fear is fear itself"
9th Academy Awards: "The Great Ziegfeld",Paul Muni&Luise Rainerwins
Prince Paul, prince regent of Yugoslavia meets withAdolf Hitlerat the Berghof, Bavaria, where he rejects deal to allow Nazi's access to Greece
15th Academy Awards: "Mrs. Miniver",James Cagney&Greer Garsonwin
United Kingdom'sPrincess Elizabeth(laterQueen Elizabeth II), joins the British Auxiliary Transport Service (ATS) as a driver
Andrei Vishinsky succeedsMolotovas Soviet Foreign minister
David Attenboroughbecomes the new controller of BBC2
John Lennonsays "We (the Beatles) are more popular than Jesus"
Worlds Ladies' Figure Skating Champion in Vienna won byPeggy Fleming(US)

Italian boxerNino Benvenutiregains the world middleweight title with a 15-round points decision over American championEmile Griffithat Madison Square Garden, NY, in the last of a famous trilogy of fights
Joe Fraziertakes his record to 20-0 and captures vacant world heavyweight boxing title; stops Buster Mathis in 11th round TKO at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Martin Luther King Jr.announces plans for Poor People's Campaign
Harold Wilsonreplaces resigningEdward Heathas British premier
Roger Sessions' 6th Symphony premieres (in fully completed form) in New York City with José Serebrier conducting the Juilliard Orchestra
"The Ordeal ofPatty Hearst", television movie directed by Paul Wendkos, premieres on CBS
PopeJohn Paul IIpublishes his first encyclical "Redemptor Hominis"
40th hat trick in NHL New York Islander franchise history, scored byMike Bossy
Robert Mugabe's Zimbabwe African National Union - Patriotic Front (ZANU-PF) comes to power, winning the parliamentary election in Zimbabwe, making Mugabe Zimbabwe's first black prime minister
Pee Wee Reese &Rick Ferrellelected to Baseball's Hall of Fame
Tennis legendMartina Navratilovaretains her WTA Tour Championship in NYC; beatsChris Evert6–3, 7–5, 6–1 for her 5th overall Championship title
First US Congress meets and declares the Constitution in effect (9 senators, 13 representatives)
Thomas Jeffersonis the first US President to be inaugurated in Washington, D.C.
Abraham Lincolninaugurated for his 2nd term as US President. The man who would assassinate him weeks later, John Wilkes Booth, is photographed attending the inauguration.

US Army mess cook Private Albert Gitchell of Fort Riley, Kansas becomes the first documented military case of Spanish flu; start of worldwide pandemic killing 50-100 million [some sources cite date as March 11]
First flight of the airship Hindenburg at Friedrichshafen, Germany
Blanche of Castile, wife of Louis VIII of France, regent to son Louis IX, born in Palencia, Spain (d. 1252)
Henry the Navigator, Portuguese Prince who sponsored Portuguese voyages of discovery, born in Porto, Portugal (d. 1460)
Francesco de Layolle, Italian Renaissance composer and organist, born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. c. 1540)
Kanō Tan'yū [Kanō Morinobu], Japanese painter (palaces, portraits), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1674)
William Dobson, English portraitist and painter, born in London (d. 1646)
John Baron Somers, English Whig, William III's chief minister (1696-1700), born in Whiteladies, Claines, England (d. 1716)
Philip Christoph von Königsmarck, Swedish soldier (lover of Sophia Dorothea of Celle), born in Stade, (d. 1694)
Jack Sheppard, English burglar and prison escapee known as "Honest Jack", born in White's Row, London (d. 1724)
Lauritz de Thurah, Danish architect and architectural writer, born in Mariager, Denmark (d. 1759)
Aert Schouman, Dutch painter and glass engraver, born in Dordrecht, Dutch Republic (d. 1792)[1]
James Waldegrave, 2nd Earl Waldegrave, British statesman and shortest-serving Prime Minister of Great Britain at 4 days (1757), born in England (d. 1763)
George Pigot, 1st Baron Pigot, British President of the British East India Company (1755-63, 1775-6), born in London (d. 1777)
Giovanni Meli, Siclian poet (Buccolica), born in Palermo (d. 1815)
Johann Heinrich Egli, Swiss composer and violinist, born in Seegräben, Switzerland (d. 1810)
Benjamin Waterhouse, American physician, co-founder of Harvard Medical School, and smallpox vaccine pioneer, born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1846)
Dieudonne-Pascal Pieltain, Flemish composer, baptized in Liège, Prince-Bishopric of Liège, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1833)
Henry Raeburn, Scottish painter (portrait painter of George IV), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1823)
Ignaz Malzat, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1804)
Charles Dibdin, British composer, author (Sea Songs), and actor, baptized in Hampshire, England (d. 1814)
Muhammad ʿAlī of Egypt, Albanian-born Pasha and viceroy of Egypt (founder of modern Egypt), born in Kaval, Macedonia, Ottoman Empire (d. 1849)
Rebecca Gratz, Jewish American educator and philanthropist, born in Lancaster, Pennsylvania (d. 1869)
Johann Rudolf Wyss, Swiss folklorist and writer (Swiss Family Robinson), born in Bern (d. 1830)
Pavel Gagarin, Russian politician, born in Moscow (d. 1872)
Karl Lachmann, German philologist and textual critic, born in Brunswick, Germany (d. 1851)
John Joseph Abercrombie, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1877)
Wijnand Nuijen, Dutch romantic and water color painter (Wrecked), born in Den Haag, Netherlands (d. 1839)
Mykhailo Verbytsky, Ukrainian Greek Catholic priest and composer (Prostachka; The State Anthem of Ukraine), born in Nadsyannia, Russian Empire (d. 1870)
Edwards Pierrepont, American jurist and lawyer, and 33rd United States Attorney General, born in North Haven, Connecticut (d. 1892)
Charles Oberthur, German composer and harpist, born in Munich (d. 1895)
Francesco Bentivegna, Italian revolutionary who led revolts in Sicily against the Bourbon rulers, born in Corleone, Sicily (d. 1856)
Jules Antoine Lissajous, French mathematician best known for the Lissajous figures and the invention of the Lissajous apparatus, born in Versailles, France (d. 1880)[1]
John Buford, American Major General (Union Army), born in Woodford County, Kentucky (d. 1863)
Elisha Franklin Paxton [Bull Paxton], American Confederate general (Stoneville Brigade at Battle of Chancellorsville), born in Rockbridge County, Virginia (d. 1863)
(Peter) Nicolai von Wilm, German-Baltic pianist and composer, born in Riga, Governorate of Livonia, Russia (d. 1911)
Paul Lacôme, French composer, born in Le Houga, France (d. 1920)
Kristian Elster, Norwegian author (And fremmed Fugl), born at Overhalla, Norway (d. 1881)
Josip Jurčič, Slovene writer (10th Brother), born in Muljava, Austrian Empire (d. 1881)
Carl Josef Bayer, Austrian chemist (invented Beyer Process to produce aluminium), born in the Duchy of Upper and Lower Silesia (now Poland) (d. 1904)
Napier Shaw, English meteorologist who introduced the tephigram and was the first to study smog, born in Birmingham (d. 1945)
Alfred William Rich, English watercolour painter, born in rural Sussex, England (d. 1921)
Toru Dutt, Indian first published woman poet (A Sheaf Gleaned in French Fields, In Ancient Ballads and Legends of Hindustan), born in Calcutta, India (d. 1877)[1]
Robert Emden, Swiss astronomer and physicist (book on Gas Spheres), born in St. Gallen, Switzerland (d. 1940)
John Henry Wigmore, American lawyer, legal scholar and expert in law of evidence, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1943)
Reginald Innes Pocock, British zoologist and authority on arachnida and myriapoda, born in Clifton, England (d. 1947)[1][2]
David W. Taylor, U.S. Navy architect (1st towing tank), born in Louisa County, Virginia (d. 1940)
Thomas Sturge Moore, English poet and playwright, born in Hastings (d. 1944)
Boris Galerkin, Russian mathematician and engineer known for the Galerkin method, born in Polotsk, Russian Empire (d. 1945)
John H. Trumbull, American politician, Governor of Connecticut (1925-31), born in Ashford, Connecticut (d. 1961)
Helen Hellwig, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1894), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1960)
Alberto Vaccari, Italian Jesuit and old testament scholar and interpreter, born in Bastida de' Dossi, Italy (d. 1965)
Enrique Larreta [E R Maza], Argentine diplomat and writer (Gloria de Don Ramiro), born in Buenos Aires (d. 1961)
Suze Groeneweg, 1st Dutch women elected to parliament (SDAP, 1918-37), born in Strijensas, Netherlands (d. 1940)
Léon-Paul Fargue, French poet (Tancrède), born in Paris, France (d. 1947)
Theodore Hardeen, Hungarian-American magician, founder of the Magician's Guild and brother of Harry Houdini, born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1945)
Alexander Fyodorovich Gedike, Russian composer and pianist, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1957)
Daniël George van Beuningen, Dutch coal merchant (Rotterdam) and art collector, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1955)
Fritz Graebner, German geographer and ethnologist, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1934)
Garrett Morgan, American inventor (gas mask and traffic signal), born in Claysville, Kentucky (d. 1963)[1]
Egbert Van Alstyne, American popular music songwriter and ragtime pianist ("In the Shade of the Old Apple Tree"; "For Your Boy and My Boy"), born in Marengo, Illinois (d. 1951)[1]
Peter D. Ouspensky, Russian esotericist, born in Kharkov, Russian Empire (d. 1947)
Bernhard Kellermann, German writer (Der Tunnel), born in Fürth, Germany (d. 1951)
Channing Pollock, American playwright, screenwriter, and critic (The Evil Thereof, Fool), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1946)
Richard C. Tolman, American physical chemist and mathematical physicist (Principles of Statistical Mechanics), born in West Newton, Massachusetts (d. 1948)[1]
Thomas Sigismund Stribling, American writer (Pulitzer Prize 1933), born in Clifton, Tennessee (d. 1965)
Todor Aleksandrov, Bulgarian revolutionary, army officer, Macedonian freedom fighter, and teacher, born in Novo Selo, Ottoman Empire (d. 1924)
Nicolae Titulescu, Romanian diplomat, government minister and former President of the League of Nations (1930-32), born in Craiova, Romania (d. 1941)
Maude Fealy, American actor and drama coach (The Ten Commandments), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1971)
Robert Emmett Keane, American stage and screen actor (The Rookie Cop; The Red Dragon), born in New York City (d. 1981)
Sam Langford, Canadian boxer (World Coloured Heavyweight Champion x 5 1910-18), born in Weymouth Falls, Nova Scotia (d. 1956)
Red Murray, American baseball outfielder (NL home run leader 1909 NY Giants), born in Arnot, Pennsylvania (d. 1958)
Paul Bazelaire, French cellist, composer, and poet, born in Sedan, France (d. 1958)
Violet MacMillan, American Broadway theatre actress (The Magic Cloak), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1953)
Jeff Pfeffer, American baseball pitcher (no-hitter Boston Doves v Cincinnati Reds 1907), born in Seymour, Illinois (d. 1972)
Knute Rockne, American College Football HOF coach, (National C'ships 1924, 29, 30 Notre Dame; record 105–12–5), born in Voss, Norway (d. 1931)
Rafaela Ottiano, Italian-born American actress (Grand Hotel, The Devil-Doll), born in Venice, Italy (d. 1942)
Oren E. Long, American politician and 10th Territorial Governor of Hawai'i (1951-3), born in Altoona, Kansas (d. 1965)
Oscar Chisini, Italian mathematician known for his work in algebraic geometry (Chisini mean), born in Bergamo, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1967)[1]
Pearl White [Victoria], American actress and stunt woman (Perils of Pauline), born in Green Ridge, Missouri (d. 1938)

(Charles) "Dazzy" Vance, American Baseball HOF pitcher (7 x NL strike-out leader; World Series ,1934 St Louis Cardinals; NL MVP & Triple Crown of Pitching, 1924 Brooklyn Robins), born in Orient, Iowa (d. 1961)
Lois Wilson (née Burnham), American co-founder of Al-Anon also known as "Lois W.", born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1988)
Bjarne Brustad, Norwegian composer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1978)
Milt Gross, American comic book illustrator (Count Screwloose), born in The Bronx. New York (d. 1953)
Rhys Gemmell, Australian tennis player (Australasian C'ships singles & doubles 1921), born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1972)
Lefty O'Doul, American Baseball HOF left fielder (MLB All-Star, World Series 1933; NL batting champion 1929, 32 NY Yankees), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1969)
Georges Dumézil, French philologist, linguist and religious studies scholar (trifunctional hypothesis on Proto-Indo-European mythology), born in Paris, France (d. 1986)[1]
Emilio Prados, Spanish poet and editor, part of the Generation of '27, born in  Málaga, Spain (d. 1962)
Charles H. Goren 'Mr Bridge', American bridge master (26 US Titles), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
Jean Joseph Rabearivelo, Malagasy-French poet, born in 1901, Antananarivo, Madagascar (d. 1937)
Harold Berens, British comedian and actor, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1995)
John Scarne [Orlando Carmelo Scarnecchia], American magician known for his card skills, born in Steubenville, Ohio (d. 1985)
William C. Boyd, American immunochemist, discovered that blood types are inherited, born in Dearborn, Missouri (d. 1983)
Chief Tahachee [Jeff Davis Tahchee Cypert], Cherokee stage and film actor, born in James Mill, Arkansas (d. 1978)
George Gamow, Ukrainian-born American nuclear physicist and writer (Big Bang Theory, DNA), born in Odessa, Russian Empire (d. 1968)
Joseph Schmidt, Austrian-Hungarian tenor and actor (Der Liebesexpreß; A Star Fell from Heaven), born in Storozhynets, Austria-Hungary (d. 1942)
Charles Rudolph Walgreen Jr., American businessman (President of Walgreens), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2007)
Georges Ronsse, Belgian road cyclist (UCI Road World C'ship 1928, 29), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1969)
Meindert DeJong Dutch-born American children's author (Shadrach), born in Wierum, Netherlands (d. 1991)
Edgar Barrier, American actor (Phantom of the Opera, Macbeth, Rocky, Cornered), born in New York City (d. 1964)
Eleanor "Sis" Daley, wife of Chicago mayor Richard J. Daley, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
Maria Branyas, 2nd oldest American-Catalan supercentenarian (117 years, 168 days), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2024)
Harry B. Helmsley, American billionaire real estate developer (purchased Empire State Building, 1961), born in New York City (d. 199)
Tancredo Neves, Brazilian politician (Prime Minister and elected President 1985), born in São João del-Rei, Brazil (d. 1985)
Afro Basaldella, Italian painter (The Garden of Hope), born in Udine, Italy (d. 1976)
Carl Marzani, Italian-born American left wing political activist, publisher and documentarian, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1994)
Ferdinand Leitner, German conductor (Zürich Opera 1969-84; Württemberg State Opera, 1947-69), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1996)
John Garfield [Jacob Garfinkle], American actor (Four Daughters, Body and Soul), born in New York City (d. 1952)
Taos Amrouche, Algerian writer (Jacinthe noir), and Kabyle singer, born in Tunis, Tunisia (d. 1976)
Gino Colaussi, Italian soccer striker (26 caps; Triestina, Juventus, Vicenza, Padova), born in Gradisca d'Isonzo, Italy (d. 1991)
Robert R. Wilson, American physicist (The Manhattan Project), sculptor and architect, born in Frontier, Wyoming (d. 2000)
Ward Kimball, American cartoonist (worked for Walt Disney), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2002)
Carlos Surinach, Spanish-American composer, most notably of ballet music (Feast of Ashes), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1997)
Giorgio Bassani, Italian writer (Botteghe Oscure), born in Bologna,Kingdom of Italy (d. 2000)
Hans Eysenck, German-born British psychologist (most frequently cited living psychologist), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1997)
Clyde McCullough, American baseball catcher (MLB All Star 1948, 53; Chicago Cubs, Pittsburgh Pirates) and coach, born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1982)
Alfred Judson Force Moody, US Army Brigadier General, WWII and Vietnam veteran, born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1967)
